mnemonics: Sanguine-Pearson sounds like 'Same Group, Permutation' to remember that it's about a group (Sanguine) defined by a permutation (Pearson), specifically the symmetric group S_n.
keyPoints: The Sanguine-Pearson group is another name for the Symmetric Group, denoted S_n.
It is the group of all permutations of a finite set of n elements.
The group operation is composition of permutations, and its order is n!.
commonMistakes: Confusing the symmetric group S_n (all permutations) with a subgroup like the alternating group A_n (only even permutations).
Forgetting that the group operation is composition, not multiplication or addition of the elements.
